prompt: Translate this English proverb into two other languages in a way that preserves the spirit, not the words: 'The best time to plant a tree was twenty years ago. The second best time is now.' Add a one-sentence note on each.
response:
# Translations of "The best time to plant a tree was twenty years ago. The second best time is now."

## 🇪🇸 Spanish
**"El mejor momento para sembrar fue cuando aún no sabías; el segundo, es antes de que se ponga el sol."**

*Note:* This version leans on the rural wisdom of sowing and the urgency of sunset, echoing the Spanish fondness for proverbs rooted in the rhythms of the land and day.

## 🇯🇵 Japanese
**「井戸を掘るなら、喉が渇く前に。今掘らぬ者は、明日もまた渇く。」**
*(Ido o horu nara, nodo ga kawaku mae ni. Ima horanu mono wa, ashita mo mata kawaku.)*

Literally: *"If you're going to dig a well, do it before you're thirsty. He who does not dig today will thirst again tomorrow."*

*Note:* Japanese proverbs often favor concrete, everyday imagery over abstract ideals, so the well replaces the tree — a classic metaphor for preparation (drawn from the saying *渇して井を穿つ*, "digging a well only when thirsty").
